definition: The nearest significant higher-time-frame liquidity level (e.g., prior swing highs/lows, previous day/week highs/lows, equal highs/lows, failure swings, fair value gaps, or consolidation extremes) that price is expected to gravitate toward. It serves as the directional target and primary profit objective, anchoring trade bias and lower-time-frame entries.
identification_rules: Mark higher-time-frame liquidity levels: previous day/week highs/lows, equal highs/lows, failure swings (unswept swing points), old swing highs/lows, fair value gaps, and consolidation range extremes.

Determine the draw direction: if price is trending (repeatedly taking previous day highs/lows), set the draw in the trend direction; if price fails to displace over a previous high/low, the draw shifts to the opposite side.

Use the most obvious, untested liquidity level on the opposing side of the current range as the draw when a reversal signature (expansion met with expansion, V-shape reversal, SMT, CSD) confirms a low/high of day.
Prefer low-resistance liquidity (untouched failure swings, equal highs/lows) as the draw; avoid using high-resistance liquidity (already swept levels).
Do not treat failure swings inside a range as valid draws until price expands away from them and prints a reversal signature.
In consolidation, the unswept side of the range is the immediate draw; the next external liquidity level beyond is the secondary draw.
Confirm the draw with the daily change in state of delivery and swing point structure (higher lows for bullish draw, lower highs for bearish).
Anticipate an opposing stop raid on an intermediate time frame before price delivers toward the draw.
Update the draw after each candle close; an inside bar does not set a new draw, continuation of prior bias.
Only the nearest higher-time-frame extreme qualifies as the true draw; lower-time-frame swing points are intermediate targets, not the draw.
When a fair value gap lies between price and the draw, it may be visited first.
Look back a maximum of three days when identifying previous day highs/lows as candidate draws.
Mark Asian, London, and New York session highs/lows as intraday targets.
Identify equal highs and equal lows (same price level touched twice or more) as liquidity pools.
Use top-down analysis from monthly/weekly to align draw direction across timeframes.
Confirm price is not in consolidation (current candle breaks previous candle high or low); otherwise draw direction is unclear.
Use standard deviation projections (-2 to -2.5) from manipulation leg to confirm high-confidence targets when overlapping with liquidity levels.
When multiple liquidity levels overlap (e.g., swing high and FVG), prioritize that area as target.
trading_implication: The draw on liquidity must be established before any entry: it defines the directional bias and the profit target. Once identified, traders look for entries (e.g., at PD arrays) in the direction of the draw after an opposing stop raid on an intermediate time frame. Intermediate swing points serve as partial targets, while the HTF draw is the final take-profit. It also filters trades: if the draw has already been taken in a prior session, no new entry is sought. A shift in the draw signals a bias reversal. Risk-to-reward should be at least 2R.
common_mistakes: Entering without first identifying the HTF draw on liquidity, leading to directionless trades.
Treating failure swings inside a range as valid draws before price expands away and confirms.
Closing the trade at an intermediate swing point instead of holding toward the actual HTF draw.
Confusing a consolidation with a reversal, expecting the draw to remain unchanged after expansion-met-expansion.
Expecting the draw on liquidity to be reached 100% of the time; price may consolidate and not hit any level.
Relying solely on lower time frames without HTF context, leading to noisy and conflicting signals.
Ignoring conflict between candle closure signals and nearby PD arrays (e.g., fair value gaps) that produce ambiguous bias.
Failing to update the draw after each candle close, resulting in stale directional bias.
Trying to fade the HTF draw (e.g., shorting into untested equal highs on a bullish bias).
Taking a fixed reward (e.g., 2R) when the draw offers significantly greater potential.
Targeting high resistance liquidity (already swept levels) instead of low resistance failure swings.
Treating an untested high/low as a valid swing point without waiting for a sweep that creates high resistance.
Entering from a discount/premium location that does not align with the draw direction.
Taking entries against the established draw direction, such as looking for longs when the draw is below.
Trading toward a draw after it has already been swept in an earlier session.
Assuming a daily bias guarantees a tradable setup every day.
Forcing a directional draw during consolidation when price fails to take either side of the range.
Selecting a draw that is not the nearest significant HTF liquidity level, leading to unrealistic targets.
Ignoring displacement and candle closures when framing the draw, relying on invalid levels.
Failing to adjust the draw when market structure shifts or new swing points form.
Overlooking internal liquidity (fair value gaps) as a valid draw when they present a nearer, clearer target.
Confusing the sweep direction — failing to require an opposing stop raid before entering toward the draw.
Targeting failure swings as protective stops rather than as draw-on-liquidity targets.
Confusing Draw on Liquidity with actual liquidity (buyside/sellside liquidity) — the former is a price target, the latter is order clusters.
Using recently formed highs/lows instead of older, untested structures.
Only looking at price touching liquidity without confirming candle closure, leading to misjudgment of next direction.
Expecting price to continue rising when it fails to break previous candle high, ignoring the opposite target (previous candle low).
Neglecting to see a stop raid in the opposite direction before reaching the draw, causing poor entry timing.
Failing to align draw targets across timeframes, causing intraday targets to conflict with HTF targets.
Blindly applying the method in consolidation, ignoring that price may oscillate without hitting prior highs/lows.
Confusing bullish and bearish OHLC sequences, mispredicting price direction.
Over-relying on a single liquidity level without looking left for more historical swing points or order blocks.
Overtrading: not every day offers a high-probability entry even with a clear draw.
merge_notes: All three input cards are from ICT school; no school conflict. The 'Education - ICT' card had a Chinese definition and some rules in Chinese; the unified definition and rules are based on the most precise English versions from the other two cards. The 'Education - ICT (Updated)' card provided more detailed identification rules and trading implications, while 'The Foundation' card added session-based targets and standard deviation projections. All nuances preserved.
